items.question_text: What governs if a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different oceanic procedures or minima for a traffic flow?

choices.text: Apply the procedure or minima prescribed in the directive or letter of agreement for that operation.
Ignore the local procedure because Chapter 8 always overrides directives and letters of agreement.
Use the offshore-airspace rules only if the aircraft is VFR.
Suspend ATC service until a national amendment is published.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-1, oceanic ATC service follows Chapter 8 except when a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima.

items.question_text: When a situation in offshore airspace is not covered by Chapter 8, what should you apply?

choices.text: Apply the relevant provisions in the rest of the Order.
Use ICAO procedures only and disregard JO 7110.65.
Stop providing service until a separate offshore supplement is issued.
Use oceanic controlled airspace procedures only, even if Chapter 8 is silent.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-2, offshore airspace service uses Chapter 8 procedures and minima, and when Chapter 8 does not cover the situation the rest of the Order applies.

items.question_text: Which operation is permitted on a VFR flight plan in Oceanic FIRs?

choices.text: A sunrise-to-sunset flight below FL180 in the Miami Oceanic CTA with weather at or above basic VFR minima.
A nighttime VFR flight below FL180 in the Miami Oceanic CTA if the pilot remains clear of clouds.
An Oakland FIR VFR flight 150 NM seaward from the shoreline inside controlled airspace.
Any VFR flight above the Oceanic CTAs as long as 14 CFR 91.155 weather exists.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight-plan operations in Oceanic FIRs are limited to listed areas, must meet basic VFR weather minima, and are permitted only between sunrise and sunset.

items.question_text: Which is an approved type of separation for these offshore and oceanic procedures?

choices.text: Vertical separation.
Visual separation whenever both crews report each other in sight.
Wake turbulence separation by itself.
Pilot self-separation when communications are delayed.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-4, approved separation consists of vertical, horizontal by longitudinal or lateral means, or radar separation where coverage is adequate.

items.question_text: Unless directed or charted otherwise, how are altitude assignments made within oceanic control areas?

choices.text: By flight level using the standard altimeter setting of 29.92 inches Hg.
By local altimeter setting using MSL altitude assignments throughout the route.
By the destination altimeter setting after the aircraft enters oceanic airspace.
By pilot discretion as long as the assigned route remains unchanged.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-5, altitude assignment within oceanic control areas is based on flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 unless directed or charted otherwise.

items.question_text: How soon must action be taken when a position report affecting separation is not received?

choices.text: No later than 10 minutes after the control estimate, unless another time is specified.
Exactly when the next required report becomes overdue.
Only after both adjacent facilities confirm the report is missing.
No later than 15 minutes after the aircraft was expected over the fix in every case.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, when a position report affecting separation is not received, action is taken no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.

items.question_text: What is the purpose of the oceanic error report procedures in JO 7210.632?

choices.text: To report gross navigation, height, and time errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification so the data can support risk modeling.
To authorize temporary reductions to oceanic separation whenever an aircraft reports turbulence.
To let controllers waive oceanic reporting requirements for high-performance aircraft.
To transfer all oceanic quality assurance reporting to flight crews instead of controllers.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-7, JO 7210.632 establishes reporting procedures for oceanic error categories and SAO verification, and the resulting data supports risk modeling for separation standard reductions.

items.question_text: When must control estimates be used?

choices.text: Whenever time-based separation minima are being applied.
Only when radar coverage is lost and no pilot estimates are available.
Only for VFR flights because IFR estimates come from the flight plan.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the first oceanic fix on course.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates may be calculated or updated in several ways, but they must be used when applying time-based separation minima.

items.question_text: Which aircraft is specifically listed as an excepted non-RVSM aircraft in oceanic airspace?

choices.text: An aircraft transporting a spare engine mounted under the wing within the Oakland, Anchorage, or Arctic FIRs.
Any passenger flight that is late and requests a more direct route through RVSM airspace.
Any aircraft operating below FL410 if the pilot reports smooth air.
Any cargo aircraft with one inoperative transponder if it remains in voice contact.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-9, one listed exception is an aircraft transporting a spare engine mounted under the wing within the Oakland, Anchorage, and Arctic FIRs, subject to the paragraph's approval and workload conditions.

items.question_text: An aircraft requests a weather deviation and conflicting traffic exists in the horizontal dimension, but you can establish vertical separation. What should you do next?

choices.text: Establish vertical separation, then issue the clearance to deviate from track.
Deny the request because any conflicting traffic requires the pilot to remain on course.
Issue the deviation first and wait to resolve separation after the aircraft starts turning.
Treat the request as routine and work other traffic first because the pilot can hold course temporarily.

items.explanation: Per 8-1-10, if conflicting traffic exists in the horizontal dimension, ATC establishes vertical separation and then issues the clearance to deviate.

items.question_text: When must a revision to an estimate be forwarded to the appropriate ATS facility?

choices.text: When the estimate changes by 3 minutes or more.
Whenever the estimate changes by any amount, even a few seconds.
Only if the receiving facility specifically asks for the new estimate.
Only for IFR flights, not for VFR flights receiving services.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-1, ARTCCs forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more to the appropriate ATS facility.

items.question_text: When nonradar separation minima are being applied, when should air-ground communications be transferred unless otherwise agreed?

choices.text: 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the boundary.
Exactly at the control boundary with no earlier coordination.
10 minutes after the aircraft enters the accepting unit's airspace.
Only after the accepting unit changes the aircraft's clearance.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-2f, when nonradar separation minima are being applied, transfer of air-ground communications is made 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the boundary unless otherwise agreed.

items.question_text: How should AIDC be used when interfacility data communications capability has been implemented?

choices.text: In accordance with regional Interface Control Documents and supported by letters of agreement between the facilities involved.
Only by verbal coordination because data communications cannot be used for ATC coordination.
At each controller's discretion without any regional document or agreement.
Only for weather coordination and never for flight plan or control data.

items.explanation: Per 8-2-3, implemented AIDC use for ATC coordination is accomplished in accordance with regional Interface Control Documents and supported by letters of agreement.

items.question_text: If two aircraft require different time-based longitudinal standards, which standard applies?

choices.text: The larger of the two applicable standards.
The smaller standard, because it creates better traffic flow.
The standard for the leading aircraft only.
Any standard the two controllers agree on verbally.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-1, when one aircraft requires a different time-based longitudinal standard than another, the larger of the two standards is applied.

items.question_text: When may vertical separation be discontinued for aircraft on reciprocal tracks?

choices.text: After both aircraft report passing a significant point and have at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal spacing, or after both report passing ground-based NAVAIDs or DME fixes showing they have passed each other.
As soon as the aircraft are estimated to pass each other, even without any report confirming it.
Only when both aircraft are radar identified by the same facility.
Immediately after one aircraft reports the other in sight.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-2b3, reciprocal-track aircraft remain vertically separated until one of the listed report-based conditions is satisfied.

items.question_text: What must ATC issue to each aircraft when Mach number technique is being applied?

choices.text: A Mach number, or when appropriate, a range of Mach numbers.
Only a groundspeed assignment in knots.
Only a crossing time at the next significant point.
A Mach assignment to the following aircraft only.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3d1, a Mach number or, when appropriate, a range of Mach numbers must be issued to each aircraft when Mach number technique is being used.

items.question_text: When may a 5-to-9-minute longitudinal minimum be used under Mach number technique?

choices.text: When the preceding aircraft is maintaining a higher Mach number than the following aircraft by the amount associated with the selected minimum.
Whenever the following aircraft is faster than the preceding aircraft by any amount.
Only when both aircraft are below FL200 and direct voice communications are unavailable.
Whenever both aircraft are turbojets on reciprocal tracks.

items.explanation: Per 8-3-3e2, minima between 9 and 5 minutes are used only when the preceding aircraft is maintaining a Mach number greater than the following aircraft by the required differential.

items.question_text: What lateral minimum applies within the Gulf Low Offshore Grid System between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints?

choices.text: 12 NM.
23 NM.
50 NM.
90 NM.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-1, 12 NM is used within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.

items.question_text: Aircraft are crossing from airspace with a smaller lateral minimum into airspace with a larger one. When may lateral separation continue to exist at the boundary transition?

choices.text: When the smaller separation exists at the boundary and the flight paths diverge by at least 15 degrees until the larger minimum is established.
Whenever the aircraft are both RNAV-equipped, regardless of divergence or spacing at the boundary.
Only if the larger minimum already exists before either aircraft reaches the boundary.
Whenever radar contact exists on at least one aircraft past the boundary.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-2a3, the boundary-crossing method requires the smaller separation at the boundary and at least 15 degrees of divergence until the larger minimum is established.

items.question_text: Which reduced protected-airspace configuration is correct at and above FL240 after the route has been flight checked and users have been notified?

choices.text: 10 miles on each side of the centerline to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then widening on a 5-degree angle.
5 miles on each side of the centerline to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then widening on a 10-degree angle.
12 miles on each side of the centerline for the entire route segment with no widening.
10 miles on each side of the centerline to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID, then widening on a 3-degree angle.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-3b, at and above FL240 the protected airspace may be reduced to 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, then increasing on a 5-degree angle.

items.question_text: For track separation on the same NDB, which condition is required?

choices.text: The tracks diverge by at least 30 degrees and one aircraft is at least 15 miles from the NAVAID.
The tracks diverge by at least 15 degrees and both aircraft are within 5 miles of the NAVAID.
The aircraft are inbound to the NDB and both crews report the station in sight.
The tracks diverge by at least 45 degrees and one aircraft is 5 miles from the NAVAID.

items.explanation: Per 8-4-4a2, same-NDB track separation requires at least 30 degrees of divergence and one aircraft at least 15 miles from the NAVAID, subject to the paragraph's inbound-track limitation.

items.question_text: When vertical separation is applied below FL180 across the offshore/oceanic boundary, what must control action protect against?

choices.text: Differences between the standard altimeter setting and the local altimeter setting eroding the separation.
Any difference between indicated airspeed and Mach number.
Variations in pilot-reported fuel endurance.
Loss of direct voice communication after the aircraft is above FL180.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-1, control action must ensure differences between QNE and QNH do not compromise separation when aircraft cross the boundary below FL180.

items.question_text: What two conditions establish course-divergence separation for aircraft entering oceanic airspace?

choices.text: The courses diverge by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established, and radar separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage.
The aircraft are assigned different altitudes and both crews can accept a visual climb.
The tracks diverge by 10 degrees and each aircraft reports the other in sight.
The aircraft are radar identified and 5 minutes apart at the boundary.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-2, separation exists when aircraft are established on courses diverging by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established and the horizontal radar separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage.

items.question_text: When may an outbound aircraft climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic during the offshore-to-oceanic transition?

choices.text: Only if vertical separation above the opposite-direction traffic is established before the boundary crossing and 15 minutes before the estimated pass.
Whenever 5 minutes exists between the aircraft at the point of climb clearance.
As soon as the outbound aircraft enters oceanic airspace, regardless of the estimated passing time.
Only after both aircraft report level at the same altitude.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-3, the climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic is permitted only when vertical separation above that traffic is established before the boundary crossing and 15 minutes before the estimated pass.

items.question_text: Which timing condition is required before using the same-direction 5-minute climb-through procedure?

choices.text: The following aircraft must commence climb within 10 minutes after passing the required exact reporting point or radar-observed position of the preceding aircraft.
The preceding aircraft must begin descent within 5 minutes after the following aircraft reaches the common point.
The following aircraft must begin climb within 15 minutes after entering oceanic airspace, regardless of the preceding aircraft's position.
The following aircraft must delay the climb until 10 minutes after passing the preceding aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-4d, the following aircraft must commence climb within 10 minutes after passing the required reported or radar-observed position of the preceding aircraft.

items.question_text: When is the radar-identification transition procedure not authorized?

choices.text: When there is not enough time to establish another approved form of separation if radar identification of the transiting aircraft is delayed or cannot be obtained.
Whenever the transiting aircraft is RNAV equipped and still outside known radar coverage.
Whenever only one of the two aircraft is in direct radio communication with ATC.
Whenever the aircraft is entering Guam, San Juan, or Honolulu CTA radar coverage.

items.explanation: Per 8-5-5h, this procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish another approved separation method if radar identification cannot be established promptly.

items.question_text: Which methods are approved for separating aircraft from a temporary stationary reservation?

choices.text: Lateral or vertical separation.
Longitudinal or visual separation only.
Radar identification of the reservation followed by pilot-avoided separation.
Altitude reservation only, because lateral methods are not authorized around stationary reservations.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-1, aircraft may be separated from a temporary stationary reservation laterally or vertically.

items.question_text: A pilot refuses an avoidance clearance for a reservation. What must you advise?

choices.text: Advise the pilot of the potential hazard and that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation.
Advise only that the reservation exists, then continue normal ATC service through it.
Advise that entry is illegal and immediately cancel the aircraft's IFR clearance.
Advise that the pilot must remain clear, but no further notification is required if the pilot refuses.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-2, if a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance, the controller informs the pilot of the potential hazard and advises that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation. If possible, the appropriate using agency is also informed.

items.question_text: How is longitudinal separation applied for a temporary moving airspace reservation?

choices.text: By clearing aircraft so the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.
By placing the aircraft exactly abeam the center of the moving reservation.
By applying a fixed 5-minute buffer regardless of reservation size or traffic type.
By keeping aircraft at least 15 degrees off the reservation's track.

items.explanation: Per 8-6-3b, longitudinal separation from a temporary moving reservation is applied by keeping the appropriate minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation.

items.question_text: When no specific exception is stated, what procedures and minima govern ATC service in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in the North Atlantic section.
Only the general Chapter 8 application paragraph, without any regional modification.
The Caribbean regional procedures because they are the default oceanic fallback.
Any procedure selected by local facility directive without regard to the section.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-1, North Atlantic ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in that section except when otherwise noted.

items.question_text: How is vertical separation provided in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.
Only by metric altitudes assigned under regional pilot discretion.
Only by Mach number technique if both aircraft are turbojets.
By applying the Pacific vertical separation table whenever radar is unavailable.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-2, vertical separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.question_text: What longitudinal minimum applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the West Atlantic Route System?

choices.text: 20 minutes.
10 minutes.
15 minutes.
30 minutes in every case regardless of location.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-3c1, 20 minutes is applied between nonturbojet aircraft operating in the WATRS.

items.question_text: What minimum reported ITP distance must exist between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft before an ADS-B In Trail Procedure climb or descent may be cleared?

choices.text: 15 NM or more.
10 NM or more.
25 NM or more in every case.
At least one degree of latitude.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-3d3, the reported ITP distance between the ITP aircraft and any reference aircraft must be 15 NM or more.

items.question_text: When may 23 NM lateral separation be used in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?

choices.text: When approved aircraft at a minimum of RNP 4, RCP 240, and RSP 180 operate in designated airspace with direct voice or CPDLC communications and required ADS-C contracts monitored by automation.
Whenever both aircraft are in NAT HLA and have any form of datalink, regardless of RNP or RSP approval.
Whenever both aircraft are non-RNP and remain west of 55 degrees West.
Only for supersonic aircraft operating above FL275.

items.explanation: Per 8-7-4a, 23 NM lateral separation requires approved aircraft, designated airspace, direct voice or CPDLC communications, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by automation.

items.question_text: When no specific exception is stated, what procedures and minima govern ATC service in the Caribbean ICAO Region?

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in the Caribbean section.
Only the North Atlantic regional procedures because the two regions share WATRS traffic.
Only local facility directives without regard to the regional section.
The Pacific regional procedures whenever ADS-C is being used.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-1, Caribbean ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in that section except when otherwise noted.

items.question_text: How is vertical separation provided in the Caribbean ICAO Region?

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.
Only by applying WATRS longitudinal rules.
Only when the aircraft are operating below FL200.
By local altimeter-setting procedures unique to each FIR.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-2, vertical separation in the Caribbean ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.question_text: What longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft operating below FL200 in the San Juan Oceanic area outside WATRS, the Miami Oceanic area, and the Houston Oceanic area?

choices.text: 20 minutes.
10 minutes.
15 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-3c, turbojet aircraft operating below FL200 in those Caribbean-region areas use 20 minutes longitudinal separation.

items.question_text: What lateral minimum applies west of 55 degrees West to aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 when they operate between the U.S., Canada, or Bermuda and points in the Caribbean ICAO Region?

choices.text: 90 NM.
50 NM.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-4d, 90 NM applies to aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 operating within WATRS or west of 55 degrees West between the U.S., Canada, or Bermuda and points in the Caribbean ICAO Region.

items.question_text: After clearing an IFR flight to climb or descend in VFR conditions in the Houston, Miami, or San Juan CTA, what must you do if VFR conditions may become impractical?

choices.text: Issue alternative instructions.
Cancel IFR automatically and require the pilot to continue VFR.
Continue the clearance unchanged because the pilot accepted the risk when requesting it.
Transfer communications immediately so the next sector can decide.

items.explanation: Per 8-8-5b1, if there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical, alternative instructions must be issued.

items.question_text: When no specific exception is stated, what procedures and minima govern ATC service in the Pacific ICAO Region?

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in the Pacific section.
Only the general Chapter 8 application paragraph with no Pacific-specific rules.
The North American ICAO Region procedures because both regions use Anchorage facilities.
Any combination of regional procedures chosen by the sector team.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-1, Pacific ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in that section except when otherwise noted.

items.question_text: How is vertical separation provided in the Pacific ICAO Region?

choices.text: In accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.
Only through ADS-C-based longitudinal spacing conversions.
Only above FL200 where Mach number technique is available.
By applying the North Atlantic HLA rules instead of Chapter 4.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-2, vertical separation in the Pacific ICAO Region is provided in accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures.

items.question_text: When using Pacific distance minima without ADS-C, how often must distance verification be obtained from each aircraft to confirm separation is still maintained?

choices.text: At least every 24 minutes.
At least every 10 minutes.
Only at each FIR boundary crossing.
Only when the aircraft are expected to reduce to 30 NM or less.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d2, when distance minima are used without ADS-C, distance verification is obtained from each aircraft at least every 24 minutes.

items.question_text: What lateral minimum applies in the Pacific ICAO Region to aircraft not covered by the 50 NM RNP provision or the 23 NM approved-aircraft provision?

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.

items.explanation: Per 8-9-4c, 100 NM is applied to aircraft not covered by the RNP-based 50 NM provision or the 23 NM approved-aircraft provision.

items.question_text: What procedures and minima govern ATC service in the North American ICAO Region?

choices.text: The procedures and minima contained in the North American ICAO Region section.
The Pacific ICAO Region procedures unless the route touches Anchorage.
Only the general oceanic application paragraph without regional supplements.
Any facility directive that mentions North Pacific traffic, regardless of section content.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-1, North American ICAO Region services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in that section.

items.question_text: Besides the standard IFR altitude assignment and verification procedures, what additional source governs vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region?

choices.text: Facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
Pilot-filed remarks showing the preferred altitude system for the route.
Only the next downstream oceanic sector's local SOP.
The aircraft manufacturer's metric-conversion guidance.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-2, vertical separation is provided in accordance with Chapter 4 IFR altitude assignment and verification plus facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.

items.question_text: When DME/RNAV longitudinal separation is applied in the listed North Pacific high-control areas, what minimum applies if separation is based on RNAV rather than DME reports or radar observations?

choices.text: At least 40 NM.
At least 30 NM.
At least 23 NM.
At least 50 NM.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d1, 30 NM may be used when DME reports or radar observations establish the distance; otherwise at least 40 NM based on RNAV is required.

items.question_text: Where may 23 NM lateral separation be applied in the North American ICAO Region?

choices.text: To approved aircraft operating within the Anchorage Oceanic CTA and Anchorage Continental CTA with direct voice or CPDLC communications and required ADS-C contracts monitored by automation, but not within the Anchorage Arctic CTA.
To any RNP-approved aircraft anywhere in the North American ICAO Region, including the Anchorage Arctic CTA.
Only to non-RNP aircraft in the Anchorage Arctic CTA where voice communications are unavailable.
Only when both aircraft are using DME/RNAV longitudinal separation in the Norton Sound high-control areas.

items.explanation: Per 8-10-4b, 23 NM may be applied to the approved aircraft described in the Anchorage Oceanic and Anchorage Continental CTAs, and it is not applicable within the Anchorage Arctic CTA.
